articles.title: Rybakina Criticizes Electronic Line-Calling After Controversial Call in Madrid
articles.summary: Elena Rybakina expressed distrust in the electronic line-calling system at the Madrid Open after a disputed call during her second-round match against Zheng Qinwen. The controversy occurred in the second set when Zheng served what was called an ace by the system, with Rybakina trailing 30-0 and 4-3. Rybakina, who ultimately won 4-6, 6-4, 6-3, pointed to a ball mark on the clay court and asked umpire Julie Kjendlie to inspect it. The umpire refused, stating she had to rely on the electronic system. Rybakina argued that the mark did not match the television replay, saying, "The system is wrong, this is not a joke." This incident echoes a similar controversy from the previous year involving Alexander Zverev, who was warned for taking a photo of a contested ball mark. Zverev had claimed a "malfunction in the system." Rybakina noted the similarity, saying the mark was "in front of her nose" and that it was frustrating to lose a point unfairly. The match continued with Rybakina overcoming the setback to advance. The incident raises questions about the reliability of electronic line-calling on clay courts, where ball marks can provide visual evidence.

articles.title: Oil Prices Surge as US-Iran Peace Talks Stall
articles.summary: Oil prices increased by about 2% after plans for a second round of peace talks between the US and Iran stalled. US President Donald Trump cancelled sending a team to Pakistan for negotiations with Iranian officials. The global benchmark Brent crude rose to $109.33 per barrel, while US crude reached $96.78. The conflict has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial waterway through which about one-fifth of the world's crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes. Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi has been engaging in discussions with Oman and is set to meet Russian President Vladimir Putin. Experts warn that if the strait remains closed for more than a few weeks, the impact on global supply chains could be severe, affecting products from bin bags to medicine. Oil traders are cautious and waiting for credible evidence of a lasting peace before reacting to headlines.

articles.title: How a War in Iran is Straining UK Family Budgets
articles.summary: The recent war between the US, Israel, and Iran, which began on February 28, has had unexpected financial consequences for families in the UK. Fuel prices have surged dramatically: diesel has risen by 35% and petrol by 19% in nearly two months, according to government data. This means filling a typical family car with petrol now costs about £14 more, while a diesel tank is £27 more expensive. For families like Naomi's, who live in Chorley, Lancashire, this increase is a significant burden. Naomi's 10-year-old daughter, Riziah, has complex medical conditions that require frequent hospital visits in Liverpool, over 30 miles away. The family uses a van for these trips, and the extra fuel cost is about £30 per week. Naomi is also concerned about rising energy bills. Riziah relies on medical devices that use extra electricity, and the house must be kept warm year-round. The energy price cap, which limits unit costs, dropped in April but is predicted to increase by 12% in July, reaching £1,843 per year for a typical household. Naomi's family is cutting back elsewhere to cope, but the financial pressure is mounting. This story illustrates how global conflicts can directly affect everyday life, even for those far from the conflict zone.

articles.title: Retired IT Analyst Wins Million Pound Jackpot on Who Wants To Be A Millionaire?
articles.summary: Roman Dubowski, a retired IT analyst from Stockport, England, has become the seventh contestant to win the £1 million jackpot on the ITV quiz show 'Who Wants To Be A Millionaire?', hosted by Jeremy Clarkson. Dubowski, who enjoys quizzing as a hobby, correctly answered the final question about a trademarked logo used since 1876. The logo was for Bass Ale, which he recognized from Édouard Manet's painting 'A Bar at the Folies-Bergère' at The Courtauld Gallery in London. After using the 50/50 lifeline, he confidently chose Bass Ale over Coca-Cola. Dubowski described the win as 'unreal' and said he needed a cup of tea to process it. He plans to buy a new house and travel to New Zealand and South America with his winnings. Earlier in the game, he experienced self-doubt when asked about ingredients for mayonnaise, but he ultimately succeeded.

articles.title: Gauff Overcomes Illness to Win in Madrid
articles.summary: Coco Gauff, the third seed from the United States, showed incredible determination as she fought back from a set and a break down to defeat Sorana Cirstea 4-6, 7-5, 6-1 in the third round of the Madrid Open. Despite battling a stomach virus that caused her to vomit into a bin during the second set, Gauff managed to rally and secure victory. She took a medical timeout and received medication that helped ease her symptoms. The win sets up a fourth-round match against Czech player Linda Noskova. Gauff is the defending champion at the French Open, which starts next month, and this tournament is part of her preparation on clay. Several other players have also been affected by illness in Madrid, including world number one Iga Swiatek, who retired from her match due to lack of energy. Meanwhile, British player Cameron Norrie defeated Thiago Agustin Tirante 7-5, 7-6(5) and will face world number one Jannik Sinner in the next round.

articles.title: Hollywood Actor Hans Obma Joins Welsh Soap After Learning the Language
articles.summary: Hans Obma, an American actor known for roles in Better Call Saul, The Vampire Diaries, and Criminal Minds, is joining the cast of the Welsh-language soap opera Pobl y Cwm. Inspired by his Welsh grandmother from Brynmawr, Obma learned Welsh to take on the role. He describes the experience as exhilarating and says Wales has become his second home. The show, which first aired in 1974 and moved to S4C in 1982, is 97% in Welsh. Obma plays an American character, so he retains his accent. He prepared by watching Welsh TV programs including Pobl y Cwm, Rownd a Rownd, and Newyddion S4C. Obma's grandmother moved to the US after World War Two and never spoke Welsh, but her accent returned when she visited Wales with him. Obma's addition to the cast highlights the show's ongoing appeal and its role in promoting the Welsh language.
